body_txt: The Trope Namer and prime example of this sort of behavior is a hypothetical scenario (first told by British philosopher Antony Flew in his 1975 book Thinking About Thinking ) in which a Scotsman reads about a scandalous crime in an English town and smugly thinks to himself, "No Scotsman would ever do such a thing." The next day he reads about an even more scandalous crime in the nearby city of Aberdeen. Rather than admit that he was wrong, he instead thinks, "No true Scotsman would ever do such a thing." In this case, he is retroactively changing his standards of what a Scotsman is from "someone who lives in Scotland" to "someone who lives in Scotland and meets my standards of 'Scottish' behavior." This is very common within subcultures. Works or creators are discredited as not part of the genre due to not living up to arbitrary standards ( or just being popular ). Often this is followed by examples of what are considered real examples of the genre (see also Pretender Diss ). By extension, you aren't considered a real fan of the genre if you don't know of these works. Sometimes, when dealing with a "dead" genre or another sufficiently ghettoized field , the fallacy is used to try and distance a well-liked entry from it. A related tactic is called gatekeeping and refers to when someone claims to be a fan of X and another person challenges them to prove they are a "real" fan of X by reciting a bunch of facts about X. In real life, it's most commonly found in arguments about politics, race, nationality , or religion. It is typically used to defend one's own side/religion/organization from criticism by claiming that any extremists or other bad people found within their ranks are not actually a part of their group, even though they are operating in their name. Oddly enough, it can be meant as a compliment within the same circles (though it won't be perceived as one ), if it is applied to a member of a different group, which as a whole is assigned negative attributes. People who use the fallacy in such contexts are not necessarily consistent. For example, they may say that the Soviet Union had true communism insofar as it did anything good but not insofar as it did anything bad. This has led one wag to coin the term "Schrödinger's Scotsman". Essentially a form of Begging the Question , in that, to accept the argument that No True Scotsman would do X, one must accept that the definition of "True Scotsman" includes "would not do X." It's also a good example of Confirmation Bias in action. Related to Moving the Goalposts , where the definition isn't changed, but the standards for accepting a counter-argument are made increasingly more rigorous.   Not related to Violent Glaswegian (the very thing this trope meant to defy), Brave Scot , The Scottish Trope , that Shcottish actor , or Fake Scot . A form of Selective Obliviousness . Straw Affiliation is a related form of this. Stop Being Stereotypical and "Not So Different" Remark are often invoked when these situations occur. The inverse is Hitler Ate Sugar , when someone tries to argue that all True Scotsmen are evil because of one bad apple, though both tropes can be (and often are) used hand-in-hand to stigmatize and exclude certain people / things / etc. noreallife It's perhaps also worth mentioning that, among those who wear the kilt , a "True Scotsman" is the humorous term for someone who is confident enough to go commando . So if someone asks "Are you a true Scotsman," it might not be this fallacy — they may just be wondering about your underwear. Not to be confused with NotScotish , a web animator.
